title: ‘Spider-Man,’ ‘The Evil Dead,’ and the Sam Raimi Rankings
format: Sean and Adam Naiman rank all fifteen Sam Raimi movies worst to best, then Sean talks with Tom Gormican about writing and directing 'The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent.'

films.title: Evil Dead II
films.note: No. 1, no argument. A profound movie made in a profoundly goofy way — basically a remake of the first film with higher-concept gags, a bigger Bruce Campbell performance, and no compromise anywhere in it.

films.title: A Simple Plan
films.note: No. 2. The most ‘movie’ movie Raimi ever made — a neo-noir about two brothers and a bag of cash that plays like the missing link between Fargo and No Country for Old Men. Bill Paxton, not Oscar-nominated costar Billy Bob Thornton, is the real find.

films.title: Darkman
films.note: No. 3. A wronged-man revenge movie more than an actual superhero movie, made cheap and shot like real comic-book panels instead of a screensaver. Frances McDormand and Liam Neeson are both terrific.

films.title: Drag Me to Hell
films.note: No. 4. Disgusting and funny in equal measure, and mean without an R rating. The movie Raimi cashed his Spider-Man chip in to make.

films.title: Spider-Man 2
films.note: No. 5. The Doc Ock surgery scene is a genuine Hitchcock/William Castle homage smuggled into a studio superhero movie, and Alfred Molina makes a potentially ridiculous villain work — Marvel is still drawing on that character today.

films.title: Army of Darkness
films.note: No. 6. Moves the Evil Dead films to the Middle Ages and Monty Pythons them, with Ray Harryhausen-style visual references. Bruce Campbell is the whole movie, and it's a one-liner factory.

films.title: The Evil Dead
films.note: No. 7. Scrappy, lo-fi horror made on nothing, comparable to The Thing as a viewing experience despite a fraction of the budget.

films.title: Spider-Man
films.note: No. 8. Buoyant rather than apocalyptic — just people who love or hate each other in New York, with stakes that don't hinge on a giant glowing cube. The one that opened the floodgates for the entire superhero genre.

films.title: The Gift
films.note: No. 9. A serious, atmospheric movie about a mystic with ESP, anchored by Cate Blanchett before she was a known name in America, with Keanu Reeves, Giovanni Ribisi, and Katie Holmes filling out the cast.

films.title: The Quick and the Dead
films.note: No. 10. A revisionist, slapstick Western with a stacked cast — Sharon Stone, Gene Hackman, a 21-year-old Russell Crowe, and Leonardo DiCaprio getting the ampersand billing. Structured like an actual gunfight tournament bracket.

films.title: Crime Wave
films.note: No. 11. The one Raimi made with the Coen brothers that all three have disavowed — an uncategorizable, weirdly retro genre parody that doesn't fully work but has real heart in it.

films.title: For Love of the Game
films.note: No. 12. A syrupy, sentimental baseball movie that doesn't work: Kevin Costner and the ballgame are good, the romance is not.

films.title: It's Murder!
films.note: Tied at No. 13. Neither host has actually seen Raimi's first short — grouped here with Within the Woods as the early, prove-yourself work of a young independent filmmaker.

films.title: Within the Woods
films.note: Tied at No. 13. The 32-minute proof-of-concept short that got The Evil Dead financed — a little boring at that length, but the primal scene of Raimi's whole career.

films.title: Spider-Man 3
films.note: No. 14. Too many ideas and too many villains (Sandman, Venom), overstuffed and overlong, and it loses the focus on Peter and Mary Jane that made the first two work. The theater was dead when it played.

films.title: Oz the Great and Powerful
films.note: No. 15, the worst. High production value that still looks bad — built more around an aspect-ratio gimmick than a real reason to exist, and unlike Tim Burton doing this kind of thing yearly, Raimi only swung at it once.

films.title: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent
films.note: Gormican and co-writer Kevin Etten wrote the entire script before Nicolas Cage had agreed to anything, hoping just to get a lunch out of it. Cage said no at first; a letter about the breadth of his career — Moonstruck to Con Air to Adaptation — brought him around.
